title: Topic 355: Why Review Bombing Happens: Consumer Activism Psychology
content:
_**Topic 355: Why Review Bombing Happens: Consumer Activism Psychology**_

**Category:** Consumer Psychology

### ABSTRACT

In the grand amphitheater of online commerce and entertainment, a curious form of gladiatorial combat has emerged: the review bomb. This paper delves into the digital trenches to understand why hordes of disgruntled consumers, armed with keyboards and a surplus of free time, descend upon product pages to carpet-bomb them with one-star ratings. Is it a righteous crusade of consumer activism, a digital-age temper tantrum, or simply what happens when you give a mouse a cookie, and that cookie is the entire internet? Through a rigorous, yet surprisingly amusing, analysis of psychological theories and real-world case studies, we explore the motivations behind this phenomenon. We argue that review bombing is a complex cocktail of perceived injustice, mob mentality, and the intoxicating thrill of collective power. This paper will not only shed light on the inner workings of the modern consumer’s mind but also provide a much-needed chuckle at the absurdity of it all.

### INTRODUCTION

Once upon a time, in a kingdom not so far away, the only way to voice your displeasure with a shoddy product was to write a strongly-worded letter to the manufacturer, a letter that would likely be used as kindling for the CEO’s fireplace. Today, we live in a digital democracy where every consumer has a voice, a platform, and an almost-unlimited capacity for righteous indignation. Welcome to the age of the review bomb, a phenomenon where the court of public opinion is always in session, and the verdict is often delivered with the subtlety of a sledgehammer.

Review bombing is the digital equivalent of a flash mob, but instead of synchronized dancing, it involves a coordinated assault of negative reviews on a product’s online listing. The targets are varied—video games, movies, restaurants, even banana slicers—but the goal is always the same: to tank the product’s aggregate score and, in doing so, send a message. But what is that message, exactly? Is it a legitimate form of consumer protest against corporate greed and creative bankruptcy? Or is it a manifestation of online mob mentality, where the line between constructive criticism and digital vandalism becomes hopelessly blurred?

This paper will embark on a quixotic quest to understand the psychology of the review bomber. We will journey through the hallowed halls of academia, consulting the great oracles of social psychology, and then immediately apply their wisdom to the ridiculousness of online arguments about video games. We will examine the cognitive biases, the emotional triggers, and the social dynamics that transform a mild-mannered consumer into a keyboard warrior on a holy crusade. From the trenches of Metacritic to the comment sections of Amazon, we will unearth the real-world examples that are as hilarious as they are illuminating. So, grab your popcorn, silence your cell phones, and prepare to be entertained and, dare we say, educated. The show is about to begin, and it’s getting a one-star review.

### LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely deaf to the cacophony of review bombing. While the term itself might not grace the hallowed pages of every psychology journal, the underlying principles have been studied for decades. The literature on collective behavior, social influence, and consumer activism provides a fertile ground for understanding this digital phenomenon. Let's take a stroll through the groves of academe, shall we? But be warned, the jargon can be thicker than a bowl of oatmeal.

One of the foundational concepts here is **deindividuation**, a term that sounds like it was invented by a committee of sociologists who were paid by the syllable. Coined by Festinger, Pepitone, and Newcomb in 1952, deindividuation refers to the loss of self-awareness and individual accountability that can occur in group settings [1]. In the anonymous or pseudonymous world of online forums and review sites, individuals are cloaked in a digital invisibility cloak, making them more likely to engage in behaviors they would shy away from in the real world, like, for example, leaving a 1-star review for a toaster because it doesn't support Bluetooth.

Building on this, we have the rich tradition of **mob psychology**, a field that has been trying to figure out why people in groups sometimes act like a flock of particularly angry geese. Gustave Le Bon, in his seminal work *The Crowd: A Study of the Popular Mind* (1895), argued that individuals in a crowd can form a collective mind, leading to a state of heightened emotionality and diminished intellectual capacity [2]. While Le Bon’s work is a bit dated (and, let’s be honest, a tad dramatic), the core idea that group dynamics can amplify emotions and override individual judgment is more relevant than ever in the age of social media. The digital pitchforks and torches of a review bomb are a modern-day manifestation of the very mob behavior that fascinated Le Bon.

More contemporary research, such as the work on **social identity theory** by Tajfel and Turner, offers a more nuanced perspective [3]. This theory posits that a person’s sense of self is derived, in part, from their group memberships. When a person identifies strongly with a group (e.g., “hardcore gamers,” “Tolkien purists”), they are more likely to conform to the group’s norms and defend it against perceived threats. In the context of review bombing, a change to a beloved video game franchise or a movie adaptation that deviates from the source material can be seen as an attack on the group’s identity, triggering a defensive, and often aggressive, response. The review bomb becomes a way of policing the boundaries of the group and reaffirming its values.

Finally, we must consider the literature on **consumer activism**. Historically, consumer activism has taken the form of boycotts, protests, and letter-writing campaigns. The digital age has simply provided a new set of tools for the same old grievances. As noted by Cantone, Tomaselli, and Mazzeo (2025), review bombing can be seen as a form of “ideology-driven polarisation,” where consumers use the review system to engage in a culture war [4]. This is not just about a faulty product; it’s about a clash of values, a battle for cultural supremacy fought in the comment sections of the internet. The review bomb, in this light, is less of a review and more of a political statement, a digital protest sign planted firmly in the virtual soil of a product page.

### ANALYSIS

Now that we’ve waded through the academic swamp, let’s get to the fun part: dissecting the mind of the review bomber. What makes a seemingly normal person, who probably flosses and pays their taxes, transform into a digital crusader armed with a thesaurus of insults? The answer, my friends, is a delightful cocktail of psychological quirks, social pressures, and the sheer, unadulterated absurdity of the human condition.

At the heart of the review bomb lies the aforementioned concept of **deindividuation**, which is a fancy way of saying that people do dumb things when they think no one is watching. The internet is the ultimate masquerade ball. Behind the veil of a username like “xX_DragonSlayer_420_Xx,” a person’s sense of individual responsibility melts away like a snowman in a sauna. This anonymity creates a psychological buffer, a get-out-of-jail-free card for social norms. When you’re part of a faceless mob, it’s much easier to justify leaving a review that reads, “This game gave my computer a virus and my dog a limp,” for a product you’ve never even used. The collective roar of the crowd drowns out the whisper of individual conscience.

This leads us directly to the crusade of the “true fan,” a phenomenon best explained by **social identity theory**. To a certain segment of the population, a video game franchise or a movie series isn’t just entertainment; it’s a core part of their identity. They are not just consumers; they are acolytes, guardians of the sacred lore. When a developer or a director dares to deviate from the established canon—by, say, introducing a female protagonist who doesn’t fit the traditional damsel-in-distress mold or suggesting that a beloved space wizard might have had a moment of self-doubt—it’s not just a creative choice; it’s heresy. The review bomb becomes a holy war, a digital inquisition to purge the infidels and protect the sanctity of the brand. The bombers are not just angry customers; they are the righteous defenders of the faith, and their one-star reviews are their sacred texts.

But how do they justify this crusade? Enter **cognitive dissonance**, the brain’s built-in PR department. It’s the uncomfortable feeling you get when your actions conflict with your beliefs. No one wants to think of themselves as a petty, vindictive troll. So, the brain does a little mental gymnastics. The review bomber convinces themselves that they are not just a person with too much time on their hands; they are a consumer activist, a freedom fighter striking a blow against corporate tyranny. They are not just spamming a comment section; they are “sending a message.” This self-justification is crucial. It transforms a temper tantrum into a noble cause, a digital riot into a consumer revolution.

And let’s not forget the simple, addictive pleasure of it all. Being part of a review bomb provides a powerful **dopamine hit**. There’s a thrill in collective action, a sense of belonging and empowerment that comes from joining a digital army. Watching a product’s aggregate score plummet in real-time provides instant gratification, a tangible sense of impact. It’s the digital equivalent of being part of a winning team, even if the game is just “who can be the most outraged.” In a world where many feel powerless, the review bomb offers a fleeting, but potent, taste of control.

### REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The theoretical underpinnings of review bombing are all well and good, but to truly appreciate the glorious absurdity of this phenomenon, we must venture into the wild and observe the review bomber in their natural habitat. The internet, in its infinite generosity, has provided us with a veritable menagerie of case studies, each more ridiculous than the last.

Our first exhibit is the now-legendary case of **_The Last of Us Part II_**. This highly anticipated video game dared to feature a female protagonist with muscles and a storyline that didn't cater to the lowest common denominator. The result? A review bomb of biblical proportions. The Metacritic page for the game became a digital battlefield, with one side decrying the game as a betrayal of everything they held dear and the other side praising it as a masterpiece. The air was thick with accusations of “forced diversity” and “SJW propaganda,” terms that are to the review bomber what “forsooth” and “hark” were to Shakespeare. The irony, of course, is that many of the most vitriolic reviews were posted within hours of the game’s release, suggesting that the reviewers had either completed a 30-hour game in record time or, more likely, hadn’t played it at all. It was a masterclass in performative outrage, a digital passion play for the terminally online.

Next, we have the curious case of the **Hutzler 571 Banana Slicer**. Yes, you read that correctly. A plastic device designed to slice a banana into uniform pieces became the unlikely target of a coordinated review campaign. The Amazon page for this humble kitchen gadget is a treasure trove of comedic genius, with thousands of reviews that have nothing to do with slicing bananas and everything to do with the surrealist humor of the internet. One reviewer warns that the slicer is not suitable for use as a home defense weapon. Another laments that it failed to save their marriage. It’s a beautiful, chaotic mess, a testament to the internet’s ability to find the absurd in the mundane. The banana slicer review bomb is a different beast from the ideologically driven crusades. It’s a form of digital improv comedy, a collective art project where the canvas is a product page and the paint is pure, unadulterated nonsense.

And who could forget the great **_Spore_ DRM controversy of 2008**? The video game _Spore_ was saddled with a particularly draconian form of digital rights management (DRM) that infuriated players. In response, they took to Amazon and other online retailers to voice their displeasure. The reviews were a masterclass in passive-aggressive protest. Instead of simply complaining about the DRM, they wrote elaborate, satirical reviews that praised the game’s many “features,” such as its ability to treat the paying customer like a common criminal. It was a brilliant act of subversive jujitsu, using the company’s own review system against it. The _Spore_ review bomb demonstrated that this tactic could be more than just a blunt instrument of rage; it could be a scalpel, a tool for witty, incisive critique.

These are but a few of the many, many examples of review bombing that grace the annals of internet history. From movies and TV shows to restaurants and even national parks, no corner of the consumer landscape is safe from the wrath of the one-star army. Each case is a unique blend of genuine grievance, mob mentality, and, in the best of times, a healthy dose of comedic genius.

### DISCUSSION

So, what does this all mean? Are we doomed to a future where every product is either a five-star masterpiece or a one-star abomination, with no room for nuance in between? The phenomenon of review bombing, as absurd as it often is, offers some fascinating insights into the modern human condition and the ever-evolving relationship between consumers and corporations. It’s a canary in the digital coal mine, a brightly-colored, highly-caffeinated bird that’s singing a song of discontent.

First and foremost, review bombing is a stark reminder that consumers are not passive recipients of corporate messaging. They are active participants in the creation of meaning and value. The rise of social media and online review platforms has given them a powerful megaphone, and they are not afraid to use it. This represents a fundamental shift in the power dynamics of the marketplace. The days of the top-down, one-to-many communication model are over. We are now in an age of many-to-many conversation, and that conversation is often loud, messy, and punctuated by a barrage of one-star reviews.

Furthermore, the review bomb highlights the increasingly blurred line between consumerism and identity. For many, the products they buy, the media they consume, and the brands they support are not just choices; they are expressions of self. An attack on a beloved brand can feel like a personal attack, a slight against one’s own values and identity. This emotional investment is the fuel that powers the engine of the review bomb. It’s what transforms a simple purchasing decision into a moral crusade. Companies that fail to understand this will continue to be blindsided by the passionate, and often irrational, reactions of their most ardent fans.

Finally, the review bomb is a testament to our innate human desire for community and belonging. In an increasingly fragmented and individualized world, the internet offers a digital campfire around which we can gather and share our stories. The review bomb, in its own strange way, is a form of community-building. It’s a way for like-minded individuals to find each other, to bond over a shared sense of outrage, and to feel like they are part of something larger than themselves. It’s a dysfunctional family, to be sure, but it’s a family nonetheless.

### CONCLUSION

In conclusion, the review bomb is more than just a digital temper tantrum; it is a multifaceted phenomenon that reflects the best and worst of our digital society. It is a testament to the power of the collective, the passion of the fan, and the absurdity of arguing with strangers on the internet. We have seen that the review bomber is not a monolithic entity but a complex character driven by a cocktail of psychological factors, from the intoxicating anonymity of deindividuation to the righteous fury of social identity theory. The review bomb is a messy, chaotic, and often hilarious form of consumer activism, a digital protest that is as likely to be aimed at a banana slicer as it is at a multi-million dollar blockbuster. As we move forward into an ever-more-connected future, one thing is certain: the consumer is armed, they are organized, and they have a lot of opinions. And they are not afraid to share them, one star at a time.

### REFERENCES

[1] Festinger, L., Pepitone, A., & Newcomb, T. (1952). Some consequences of de-individuation in a group. *Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology, 47*(2, Suppl), 382–389.

[2] Le Bon, G. (1895). *The crowd: A study of the popular mind*. T. Fisher Unwin.

[3] Tajfel, H., & Turner, J. C. (1979). An integrative theory of intergroup conflict. In W. G. Austin & S. Worchel (Eds.), *The social psychology of intergroup relations* (pp. 33–47). Brooks/Cole.

[4] Cantone, G. G., Tomaselli, V., & Mazzeo, V. (2025). Review bombing: ideology-driven polarisation in online ratings: The case study of The Last of Us (part II). *Quality & Quantity*, *59*, 315–341.
